metadata.title: When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable? News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of Racism

abstract: Since the U.S. 2016 presidential election, journalists and news organizations have been forced to confront shifting racial, social and political climates, and re-evaluate practices and norms. However, news coverage of racism is complex, especially because the conceptualization of racism in society is discordant, and the parameters of racism are heavily debated. News coverage can contribute to this debatability, specifically when it presents issues of racism with certain linguistic and topical features. In a content analysis of social media posts from six of the Facebook pages maintained by national broadcast and newspaper organizations, the present study explores contextual and linguistic representations of racism, and how social media users on Facebook engage with news posted by these organizations. Results suggest representations in news coverage signal a public debate about what is and is not racism. Coverage heavily emphasized prominent figures, while social media audiences amplified Trump's presence in social networks.

paragraphs: "Why don't they go back and help fix the totally broken and crime infested places from which they came," U.S. President Donald Trump said in a tweet in 2019-the "they" is referring to a group of congresswomen of color. This was not the first or the last comment or action from Trump or others that would stir conversations about race and racism in the public and media. Some news organizations referred to this statement as racist, while others notably stayed away from direct use of the word. Critique of this coverage prompted a metadiscourse about the use of the word "racist" in journalism (e.g., Allsop 2019;Fahri 2019). The oft-highly regarded Associated Press (AP) signaled the seriousness of this metadiscourse when it updated its stylistic guidelines after the event: "Do not use racially charged or similar terms as euphemisms for racist or racism when the latter terms are truly applicable."

While this change in protocol appears progressive, the guidelines create ambiguity. The "true applicability" of racism can be difficult to pin down, because society lacks consensus on the meaning of the term and the concept. The debate about the conceptualization and operationalization of racism goes well beyond the field of journalism. Ideological, political and popular positions all play into the shifting ways that people, including journalists, see and recognize racism. Not only does it lack a universal definition, racism continues to dramatically evolve over time to reflect political and societal norms. In the United States, recent iterations of conceptualizations of racism must navigate colorblind ideological positions that promote the false idea of a post-racial era (e.g., Barnes 2016;Carbado and Gulati 2013). The boundaries of racism's definitions continue to be debated and reimagined in scholarship across disciplines (e.g., Cohen 1992;Lentin 2016;Omi and Winant 1986). With little unanimity, narratives of racism as a topic of debate have become more prominent, and the deliberation of racism's boundaries supports what Titley (2019, 8) calls the debatability of racism. The debatability of racism is the "incessant, recursive attention to what counts as racism and who gets to define it."

With this in mind, the present study explores journalists' use the terms racism or racist and considers how coverage might contribute to the debatability of racism. News on social media is the focus of this research, allowing for the consideration of the complex social media environment and the behavior of social media users. Though news diets vary among age groups, social media remain popular venues for news retrieval, particularly among younger audiences (Shearer 2018). Social media also offer the researcher opportunities to unobtrusively explore audience interactions and engagement with news coverage. At the most basic level, interactions can contribute to the increased distribution of some narratives over others. Interacting with news coverage also allows users to indicate to their own user networks, to the social platform's algorithm and to newsrooms that a particular topic is important. Tracking user engagement with news is thus an important consideration for better understanding how the great racism debate narrative is amplified in social media spaces.

Building on Titley's (2016) understanding of the debatability of racism while also considering the nuances of the social media environment, this research identifies how linguistic indicators and topic emphasis prevalent in journalistic coverage and narrative decisions can contribute to the debatability of racism, while also considering how social media audiences engage with news. Analyzing the Facebook posts from six major U.S. broadcast and newspaper organizations from 2017 to 2019, this work explores how journalism signals the debatability of racism through subjectivity cues, denial discourse, and topical confinement, and how social media audiences connect and engage with these narratives. Findings show that, despite trade critiques, journalists directly use the terms racism or racists about half the time. The coverage contributes to the debatability of racism by associating it with most explicitedly in historical contexts rather than others, and by providing elites a platform for denials of racism. Results from this study indicate that social media audiences were most engaged with news related to racism and Trump, and less engaged with news about racism over time. The inclusion of social media interactions in the present study provides a more expansive look at patterns of news representation and how social media interactions might have amplified or depressed coverage patterns.

paragraphs: The Debatability of Racism Media play a vital role in informing publics and affecting public opinion. The representation of any issue, concept, or event matters immensely. Like many social issue topics, covering racism can be tricky for journalists, because the concept of racism and its influence on society are complex, and there is no one agreed upon definition or conceptualization among scholars or the public. Miles (1989) wrote that "the boundary and meaning of the concept of racism is the site of theoretical struggle." Indeed, these boundaries have long been debated both by scholars and in public discourse (e.g., Ahmed 2012;Lentin 2016;Omi and Winant 1986). Variances in philosophies of racism are typically concerned with the conceptualization of racism as a belief, an action or an institutional power, though the definition of racism is further complicated by the intentions of actors, the level of casualness or covertness, and the epistemological stance about the legitimacy of racism (e.g., Ahmed 2012). Temporal considerations are also central to the great racism debate. Historical boundary lines aid in the ostracization of contemporary individuals engaging in overtly racist ideas and actions, forwarding an idea that race and racism are concepts that properly belong to the past.

But racism and race did not disappear. In recent years scholars have rushed to explain how racism has re-manifested itself in our society amid a growing public and elite discussion about entering a "post-racial era" (e.g., Moody-Ramirez and Dates 2013). New forms of racism, like neoliberal racism, discredit racism as a legitimate concept to invoke in current public debates and push forward an idea of a post-racial society (e.g., Goldberg 2006Goldberg , 2009)). The evolution of racism now includes forms that have been variously designated as "new racism" (Barker 1981), "modern racism" (e.g., Entman 1990) and "everyday racism" (Essed 1991). Each of these concepts in turn can be considered to be manifestations of "subtle racism" (Elliot 2011) or "aversive racism" (Dovidio and Gaertner 2010), based on analyses that characterize racism's modern rhetoric as focused on the elevation of cultural differences rather than genetic and biological differences. However, Lentin (2014) argued against the "newness of the 'new racism' thesis," illustrating that the biological components and consequences of racism remain intact. Lentin (2014Lentin ( , 1281) ) noted:

The difference between racism as racial science or under Nazism and today's cultural racism is that, whereas the former imputed an unseen genetic 'racial code' (Hall 1997) from cultural signifiers, the latter does not need the genetic argument to essentialize cultural differences.
This perspective places historical racism and today's racism onto an unbroken continuum, pushing against "post-racial" positions and notions of historical boundaries.

Given the spread of discourse across many disciplines in academia and the disconcerting narratives introduced by myths of the United States as a post-racial society, public debate about the concept, relevance and legitimacy of racism is of little surprise. Ultimately, the present research inquiry does not seek to better (re)define what racism is, but instead to survey the press's contribution to the deliberation of contemporary racism. Titley (2016) argues the debate about racism is a prominent narrative that ultimately prioritizes "what counts as racism and who gets to define it," instead of the devastating effects racism has on people. The present research uses the debatability of racism as a framework for interrogating the press's contribution to public knowledge about racism, identifying how journalists represent this term in their selection of topics and presentation styles.

paragraphs: Aside from trade discourse, there have been to date few research inquiries exploring how racism is defined and presented in news (for an exception see Pantti et al. 2019). However, decades of scholarship investigating the reporting patterns in the representation of race-related news offer important clues. Hall (1978) theorized that there are structural influences in the media that contribute to the maintenance of racism in society. Misrepresentation of racial minorities tends to be where journalism's struggle with reporting race is most apparent (e.g., Dixon and Azocar 2007;Kilgo and Harlow 2019;Larson 2006). However, less scholarship has specifically centered the representation patterns of news about racism.

From an industry perspective, the direct use of the terms racist or racism in traditional media coverage can present challenges, by defying norms and introducing liability. Labeling something or someone as racist and/or racism can be emotionally triggering for the target, and journalists are therefore vulnerable to legal issues if the term is used. Avoiding direct use of the term helps avoid accountability (Allsop 2019). Neoliberal racism and post-racial positions invite and thrive on discussions about the credibility of claims of racism (e.g., Goldberg 2009), the denial of its tenets (Lentin 2016), the question of its relevance (e.g., Omi and Winant 1986), and the boundary lines of topical legitimacy (Pantti et al. 2019). Each of these components enhances the debatability of racism. The present study considers some of the stylistic and narrative representations that might signal this debatability when journalists use the terms racism or racist. Based on this literature, I argue debatability is triggered in journalism by at least three key presentations: subjectivity markers (marked opinion articles, external attributions, qualifiers, scare quotes), discussion of non-racism (denials and reverse racism), and topical confinement (relying primarily on historical representations to explain racism). These three aspects are central to this research examination. Subjectivity cues. Subjectivity cues are linguistic cues that serve to avoid accountability for the author and deflect from direct use of the term racism or racist. For example, topics of racism often appear embedded in editorials and opinion pieces. This branch of the journalistic landscape is where subjectivity is presumed, even if the piece is written by experts. Opinion articles allow for the discussion of racism to be positioned within the framework of a debate. Opinion, editorial and commentary news are often marked as such at the beginning of an article. Other subjectivity cues may be embedded within articles. One such cue is the use of linguistic qualifiers-such as prefacing racism with the term alleged-which indicate that a final decision or judgement has not yet been made. External attributions also assign subjectivity by allowing the term to enter public debate through sources, giving these sources the ultimate responsibility for defining racism. Instead of describing an action as racist, journalists might instead use quotes from others or paraphrase sources. The use of scare quotes (e.g., What he said was "racist") also signals debatability by distancing the author from the face value of the word. In the context of race and racism, Allsop (2019) noted that scare quotes used to describe racist acts or racism reduce accountability for journalists as well as those that are involved in the incident.

Opinion articles allow for the discussion of racism to be positioned within the framework of a debate. Opinion, editorial and commentary news are often marked as such at the beginning of an article. Other subjectivity cues may be embedded within articles. One such cue is the use of linguistic qualifiers-such as prefacing racism with the term alleged-which indicate that a final decision or judgement has not yet been made. External attributions also assign subjectivity by allowing the term to enter public debate through sources, giving these sources the ultimate responsibility for defining racism. Instead of describing an action as racist, journalists might instead use quotes from others or paraphrase sources. The use of scare quotes (e.g., What he said was "racist") also signals debatability by distancing the author from the face value of the word. In the context of race and racism, Allsop (2019) noted that scare quotes used to describe racist acts or racism reduce accountability for journalists as well as those that are involved in the incident.
sections.preview_head: Aside from trade discourse, there have been to date few research inquiries exploring how racism is defined and presented in news (for an exception see Pantti et al. 2019). However, decades of scholarship investigating the reporting patterns in the representation of race-related news offer important clues. Hall (1978) theorized that there are structural influences in the media that contribute to the maintenance of racism in society.

paragraphs: Narratives about what racism is not also contribute to racism's debatability (Lentin 2016). This study includes denials of racism and reverse racism. The popular perception of the moving boundaries of racism is reinforced when denials and counterarguments are treated as newsworthy. Denials often encompass both the direct denial (I'm not a racist) and the denial through deflection (I'm the least racist person on the planet). Denials ultimately grant attention to counter explanations (or distracting rebuttals), and in turn offer the public new conceptualizations of racism. "Reverse racism," which individualizes power dynamics and typically is used to describe people of color as aggressors against white people, provides a concept of racism devoid of considerations of societal and institutional power dynamics, also contributing to the debatability of racism. Reverse racism discussions ignore the power of the systematic implementation of institutional racism.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: Though institutionalized racism appears in every facet of contemporary reality, the debatability of racism is also fueled by confining the discussion of racism to the context of history, and particularly to historical acts of physical violence, deflecting analysis of more modern incidents. Goldberg (2006) argues that applying historical boundaries to the phenomenon of racism is a function of a discourse that seeks to shame those who engage in overly racist actions. By using this discourse, individuals are able both to discuss racism as a characteristic of a singular person or action, thus avoiding collective responsibility, and consolidate socially unacceptable forms of racism (e.g., racist-motivated violence, racial slurs) into a package assigned the past, thus compartmentalizing what collective responsibility has not already been eliminated.

In journalism, an overreliance on direct references to racism in news about historical events might contribute extensively to the debatability of racism. In their recent study of Finnish news media and online audience discourse, Pantti et al.'s (2019) topical analysis found that news media emphasized historical and international aspects of racism more than did online public discourse. In line with this approach, the present study examines whether direct references to racism remain most prevelant in topics of history, or if the current dynamics of the changing political and social landscape, including the national increase in hate crimes, shifts this pattern.

In response to critiques of journalism and the journalism field's own shifting procedures, this study considers the patterns of debatability introduced in journalistic discourse by examing three key markers that contribute to the modern debatability of racism in public discourse: subjectivity cues, denial discourse and historical confinement.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: News coverage in social media adds another layer of complexity to our understanding of the media's contribution to the construction of discourse about racism. Bélair-Gagnon et al. (2014, 1072) noted that "emerging networks add new complexity to a public sphere characterized by historical divisions and diversity." Undeniably, social media networks add immense complexity to the conceptualization of and discourse about racism, from the distribution of racist rhetoric through social media venues to the engagement of audiences with coverage about racism. The present study explores how news topics and presentations of racism might trigger shifts in social media interactions, and thus provides a more holistic view of press patterns on social media as well as clues about how audiences interact with news coverage.

By interacting with posts, audiences (and algorithms) play a significant role in the distribution of content across social media networks. Algorithms function alongside user input, previously programed software, and the hardware available. However, many other scholars have argued that algorithms cannot and must not be separated from the actors that create them. Noble (2018) asserts that the algorithms of search engines privilege capitalistic and private interests, and thereby reinforce racism. When it comes to trending news topics on Facebook, specifically, Carlson (2018, 18) found that the site "leans heavily on algorithms not as powerful actors but as tools that actualize user preferences." In other words, user engagement is a key tool for the personalization and prioritization of content. However, Bucher (2018) argues that Facebook's algorithm privileges certain forms of user engagement, creating visibility politics that are based on user popularity. Social media users hold significant power, and in various circumstances users have circumvented the traditional news gatekeeping process, set the news agenda, and/or directly influenced journalistic practice. By engaging and sharing on social media, users have influenced and interrupted news production processes and distribution models (e.g. Trilling et al. 2017). These studies suggest that traditional typologies of news value and eye-catching news content are not always primary indicators of audience interest. Topic, time and location can also affect social media engagement patterns (Rudat et al. 2014). Emotional triggers, surprise or unexpectedness of events can also spark engagement (Bright 2016). Considering the connection between populist politics and the coverage of racism (Pantti et al. 2019), prominence-a news value that indicates news is reportable because prominent and powerful figures are involved-is also a likely indicator of social media news engagement.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: Interested in understanding journalism's contribution to the debatability of racism while also considering the relationship to and dynamics of social media outlets like Facebook, this research considers journalistic coverage and social media engagement together, seeking to identify how news coverage shared to social media contributes to the debatability of racism. How does the presentation of racism in the news (including factors such as subjectivity cues, denial discourse, and topical considerations) contribute to our understanding of the debatability of racism and modern scholarship that seeks to discuss racism's parameters?

Audiences can amplify certain press patterns by engaging with news content on social media. Previous studies have considered a variety of different engagement factors, such as comments. This study specifically emphasizes how interactions increase the attention and distribution numbers of particular narratives in an effort to better understand how engagement with news coverage is influenced by topics, subjectivity markers, and denial discourse.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: The questions and hypotheses proposed for this study were answered with an analysis of posts from the main Facebook pages of three broadcast news organizations (ABC, NBC, CBS) and three of the most circulated newspapers in the United States (The New York Times, USAToday, Wall Street Journal). These organizations also had regularly updated and highly subscribed Facebook pages, ranging from about 5.9 million page likes (CBS News) in 2019 to more than 6.9 million page likes (The New York Times). This sample included the major news networks with national nightly news broadcasts, and three of the five most circulated newspapers in the United States (Agility PR Solutions 2020). Using boolean search terms "racism" and "racist," a final sample of 1644 posts (42.1% newspapers, 57.9% broadcast) were collected from 1 January 2017 until 31 December 2019. The unit of analysis was each individual post.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: The topics in the codebook were inductively developed using the text from posts, and then refined to include visuals for final coding. A computer-assisted approach was utilized to identify key topics. Topical patterns were extracted using the N-Gram feature of Microsoft Azure Machine Learning Studio. First, the data was preprocessed, with stop words removed and lemmatization performed. In addition, special characters and numbers were removed, and contractions were expanded. Then, text was blocked into features weighted using TextRank for graph ranking (Mihalcea and Tarau 2004). TextRanking provides similar results to approaches such as topic modeling LDA by accounting for co-occurrence and proportion. The featurization yielded a total of 72 categories that were then theoretically and practically condensed. These categories were used as a guide to construct the final codebook, which was also informed by key actors and events during the time period analyzed and a review of a subsample of visuals (n = 200).

The final codebook consisted of the refined list of topical considerations, subjectivity and denial discourse cues. Once finalized, the author of this study trained two undergraduate coders to acquire the final data. After two extensive coder training sessions, initial reliability testing was conducted (n = 80). After coding was concluded, final reliability testing was conducted on 50 posts. Using Krippendorff's alpha to assess reliability scores, final reliabilities ranged from .842 to 1.0.

Coders identified if scare quotes were used with the term racism or racist (e.g., "racism"). Coders also identified if there was outside attribution of the term, by identifying if the word was used within a direct quote or indirectly quoted someone else (e.g., "Smith said the attack was racist."). The opinion code included only posts marked explicitly as opinion or commentary. Coders also identified use of qualifiers including alleged, its equivalents, and similar terms like reportedly and supposedly.

paragraphs: Coders identified if the post was related to any of ten contextual topics and four actor-oriented topics. Featurization categorizations were available to coders to assist in coding validation. Contextual topics included mentions of white privilege (or general discussions of white people's specific role in power dynamics) or white supremacy (including mentions of white supremacist organizations like the KKK or Nazis). Coders identified if the news post discussed blackface, including the general discussion of blackface or blackface minstrelsy, reports of people using blackface/brownface, or discussion of racist costumes or attire. Posts were coded as related to anti-Black racism (e.g., mass incarceration, Confederate statues, food deserts, Black-specific racist comments), and anti-immigration racism (e.g., "shithole" countries, "go back to your country" and other immigration-specific racist comments). Antisemitism included direct mentions of antisemitic behavior, hate crimes against Jewish people (mentions of Nazis and neo-Nazis were coded under white supremacy unless antisemitic actions or crimes were specifically mentioned). Posts about racism's intersections with sexism and women's issues were also included; coders looked for the explicit intersection, not simply the mention of women victims of racism or women who are racists. Coders also identified if the topic was about a non-U.S. country (foreign), or if the post included historical contexts of racists/racism. Lastly, coders identified if the post was about an incident at a university or school. Topics were not mutually exclusive; data could account for topic overlap. For example, posts about Trump's derogatory references to people immigrating from African nations and Haiti were coded as both anti-immigrant and anti-Black affiliated.

Topics were also classified by mention of prominent figures engaged in racist events. Coders first identified if the individuals were mentioned at all, then identified if they were engaged in racist acts or being accused of such. Coders identified if the post was about Trump at all (i.e., any post that mentions Trump). Then, coders would identify if the post discussed Trump's direct affiliation with racism, such as a discussion of one of his derogatory tweets or policy decisions. Only the measure recording the direct affiliations was included in this study. In a similar fashion, coders also looked for the following groups of people: other elected political figures, military/ police, and entertainment celebrities (Trump was not included as an entertainment celebrity).

sections.header_norm: source driven racism elites and debatability
paragraphs: Results revealed that journalists' sources had a heavy influence on the visibility of discussions of racism in news coverage on social media. In almost half of coverage, racism was mentioned as part of an external attribution (45.8%). The literal discourse about racism among sources might thus be considered newsworthy. In other words, when certain people talk about the issue of racism, it gets covered (Table 1). Importantly, elite discourse (by politicians and celebrities) dominated external attributions. Data suggest prominence's role as a news value is heightened in this genre of news; news about racism is still very much connected with elite discourse (van Dijk 1993). Trump was central to this discussion, appearing in about a quarter of coverage (21.5%), followed by celebrities (12.20%) and other politicians (11.20%). Each year in the sample showed an increase in racism-related news embedded in news about Trump [χ 2 (2) = 12.68, p < .01] and politicians [χ 2 (2) = 29.82, p < .05]. Celebrities and politicians like Actress Rosanne Barr, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Trump (among others) were covered primarily because of controversial comments and tweets that often led to more sensational stories. One benefit of the correlation with elites is the general attention to issues related to racism among powerholders. From another perspective, the emphasis on prominent figures and racism could have the negative effect of undercutting or trivializing the pervasive impact of racism in society.

Unsurprisingly, elite involvement in the news helps us understand how the press maintains the debatability of racism in society. Coverage is the least likely to address racism directly when Trump and other politicians are involved, and several subjectivity cues are more likely to appear (see Table 2). For coverage of politicians (sans Trump), scare quotes, external attributions and denials of racism were more likely to be utilized to discuss racism (Table 3). Denials of racism accounted for an impressive 42.20% of news coverage analyzed, and increased over time. In 2019, denials of racism appeared in 65.8% of coverage, indicating coverage paid more attention to racism denials than to racism itself. A closer look at the data suggests this denial of racism was mostly discourse from elites, powerful politicians and celebrities attempting image repair, fulfilling the goal of distracting from the immediate issue of racism, and thereby contributing to debatability (Lentin 2016). In 2019, denials of racism appeared in 65.8% of coverage [χ 2 (2) = 355.72, p < .001]. In this year, journalists paid the most attention to racism denials in social media coverage.

Data on attention to racism denials and lack of attention to reverse racism provide additional insight about media's contribution to the debatability of racism. The heavy emphasis on denials might be a result of media norms and routines intended to achieve balance, an example of the effects of both-sidism or false equivalencies. For example, in 2019 coverage of commentary by U.S. politician Elijah Cummings, many news posts also included President Trump's response, in which he proclaimed himself to be the "least racist person anywhere in the world." This introduction of false narratives into public discourse under the guise of covering "both sides" ultimately provides them with legitimacy. Noting differences in narratives around the 2016 U.S. political election, Patterson (2016) said, "The press historically has helped citizens recognize the difference between the earnest politician and the pretender. Today's news coverage blurs the distinction." The press's emphasis on denials, especially in the context of Trump, further contributes to the blurring of this distinction.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: The term racist or racism was used explicitly by journalists in just over half of coverage (50.9%) and the proportion of direct use was consistent over time. In other words, even as discussion of racism and politicians increased over time, mentions of explicit racism did not. The metadiscourse about use of the term racism ultimately did not shift the amount of coverage that would explicitly use it. Neither did the global increase in hate crimes. This consistency in this feature indicates that the AP injunction and related editorial discourses urging journalists to "call racist things racist" likely did not result in an immediate increase in use of the term. The very debate of how journalists should discuss racism might have only contributed further to the debatability of racism rather than leading to a reckoning with traditional journalistic practices.

As shown in Table 2, historical discussions as well as discussion of blackface incidents increased the odds that coverage would directly mention racism. This suggests that news coverage on Facebook reinforces the idea that blackface is connected with a historical boundary and is relatively ostracized. By explicitedly acknowledging racism more often within historical boundaries than occurances in everday life, the press contributes to modern racism (Goldberg 2009).

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: Racism's newsworthiness did not lie in its constant and consistent effect on society. The data tell a story of episodic interest in racism's effects, and a limited interest in racism's source. Increases in coverage can be tied to key events, which explain the waning and waxing of press interest over the three-year period analyzed in this study (Table 1). Post-Charlottesville and after the NFL protests fizzled in terms of newsworthiness, anti-Black racism coverage significantly decreased in 2019. Anti-immigration racism had a small uptick in press attention in 2018, corresponding with the highly debated family separation policy and Trump's racist remarks about African countries. However, mentions of white supremacy and white privilege weren't central to coverage about racism. White supremacist groups were discussed most often in 2017 after the deadly white supremacist rally in Charlottesville, North Carolina, but attention to these groups gradually died. White privilege's connection with racism also appeared infrequently in posts, accounting for only 2.5% of mentions, but the subject had increased odds of appearing in opinion pieces-more than 1000% more likely (Table 3). By minimizing narratives about white supremacy and white privilege and their connection to racism, the press contributes to an understanding of whiteness that renders it invisible. In other words, news narratives reinforce the idea that "whiteness never has to speak its name, never has to acknowledge its role as an organizing principle in social and cultural relations" (Lipsitz 1995, 369).

Interacting with coverage. When social media audiences engage with news coverage through traceable interactions, they signal the topic's importance to others in their network, to the algorithm that drives recirculation, and to newsrooms that so often use metrics as evaluative tools. Results show that Facebook audiences were most likely to engage with racism-related news coverage involving Trump (see Table 4). This is likely both a result of audience interest and user input (Carlson 2018) and the algorithmic prioritization that promotes the visibility of some topics over others (Bucher 2018). Because Trump's coverage includes significantly more mentions of racism with subjectivity cues, it follows that social media engagement might exacerbate racism's debatability among social media publics, while also contributing to the political incivility, hate speech and polarization that is characteristic of social media spaces.

From a chronological perspective, results show that coverage circulation and narrative amplification plays out in the following way: when coverage of racism includes Trump, journalists are more likely to cover the topic, the coverage is more likely to represent racism as debatable; and social media audiences are more likely to interact with it. Because the relationship between social media audiences and journalists is multidirectional, these results suggest that media audiences and newsrooms contribute significantly to turning up the "noise" of racism's debatability (Titley 2016), particularly in the context of Trump (but not all politicians).

Results show that Facebook audiences were most likely to engage with racism-related news coverage involving Trump (see Table 4). This is likely both a result of audience interest and user input (Carlson 2018) and the algorithmic prioritization that promotes the visibility of some topics over others (Bucher 2018). Because Trump's coverage includes significantly more mentions of racism with subjectivity cues, it follows that social media engagement might exacerbate racism's debatability among social media publics, while also contributing to the political incivility, hate speech and polarization that is characteristic of social media spaces.

From a chronological perspective, results show that coverage circulation and narrative amplification plays out in the following way: when coverage of racism includes Trump, journalists are more likely to cover the topic, the coverage is more likely to represent racism as debatable; and social media audiences are more likely to interact with it. Because the relationship between social media audiences and journalists is multidirectional, these results suggest that media audiences and newsrooms contribute significantly to turning up the "noise" of racism's debatability (Titley 2016), particularly in the context of Trump (but not all politicians).

Racism's newsworthiness did not lie in its constant and consistent effect on society. The data tell a story of episodic interest in racism's effects, and a limited interest in racism's source. Increases in coverage can be tied to key events, which explain the waning and waxing of press interest over the three-year period analyzed in this study (Table 1). Post-Charlottesville and after the NFL protests fizzled in terms of newsworthiness, anti-Black racism coverage significantly decreased in 2019. Anti-immigration racism had a small uptick in press attention in 2018, corresponding with the highly debated family separation policy and Trump's racist remarks about African countries. However, mentions of white supremacy and white privilege weren't central to coverage about racism. White supremacist groups were discussed most often in 2017 after the deadly white supremacist rally in Charlottesville, North Carolina, but attention to these groups gradually died. White privilege's connection with racism also appeared infrequently in posts, accounting for only 2.5% of mentions, but the subject had increased odds of appearing in opinion pieces-more than 1000% more likely (Table 3). By minimizing narratives about white supremacy and white privilege and their connection to racism, the press contributes to an understanding of whiteness that renders it invisible. In other words, news narratives reinforce the idea that "whiteness never has to speak its name, never has to acknowledge its role as an organizing principle in social and cultural relations" (Lipsitz 1995, 369).

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: With journalism's long history of maintaining the status quo when it comes to racial inequality (e.g., Hall 1978;Herman and Chomsky 1994;Kilgo and Harlow 2019), this study explored how news organizations contribute to maintaining the debatability of racism (and thereby the stifling of racial progress). When covering Trump and politicians, journalists tended to dial up subjectivity cues to avoid accountability; direct mentions of racism were most likely to appear in historical contexts; and discourse about racism's source-white privilege and white supremacy-infrequently made headlines. Importantly, as journalists debated the presentation of racism within news coverage, the subjectivity cues at the heart of this discussion, such as scare quotes and qualifiers, were included infrequently. Additionally, the Associated Press style guide changes, news organization editorial policy changes, and metadiscourse of 2019 did not seem to create drastic changes in journalistic practice. Journalism continued to contribute to the contestation of racism's boundaries. For journalists and news organizations, these findings suggest the need for the industry-wide examination and adoption of standards about reporting racism and racists. If the debatability of racism is defined by "what counts as racism and who gets to define it" (Titley 2016), then this study suggests that history counts as racism, and sources, especially politicians, get to define (or blur) its meaning. Social media audiences, in conjunction with algorithms, are disrupting some of the patterns identified in this study, but that may not be enough to dissolve the influence news media can have on sustaining narratives of debatability. Continued exploration of digital and social media press narratives must assess audience engagement alongside coverage patterns to get a more accurate sense of press circulation and address trends and issues in media representation in those spaces.

sections.paper_id: Brown - 2021 - When is the “Racist” Designation Truly Applicable News Media’s Contribution to the Debatability of
paragraphs: The limitations of this study provide room for reassessment and areas for future work. This study focused on coverage that used the term racist or racism; other avoidance strategies are important to consider in future work. The conclusions are drawn from data collected from the coding of news coverage posted to Facebook by six news organizations in the United States. Some findings nevertheless affirm previous work exploring press coverage in other countries (e.g., Pantti et al. 2019). Further exploration of coverage from a diversified landscape of news organizations, especially those with partisan or alternative ideological positions, is warranted to expand the generalizability of findings and understand how the debatability of racism is furthered along political lines.

Like Pantti et al.'s (2019) research in Finland, these data show that coverage rarely discussed reverse racism. Because the present study only coded the content present in news as posted on Facebook, and not the entirety of the article, this research cannot rule out the fact that the topic of reverse racism may appear later in news coverage. The results do suggest that within the click-and-scan environment prominent on digital and social media, this part of the debate does not take center stage. But as Pantti et al. (2019) showed, reverse racism remains a prominent part of public discourse, despite its rare appearance in news coverage. Future studies might explore if there are specific features of racism/racist-related coverage that prime people or journalists to discuss reverse racism.

In addition, considering other social media platforms would aid in a more comprehensive understanding of news distribution on social media and news audience engagement. This study shows that certain topics are covered differently, and social media audiences are more likely to interact with certain topics and narrative features than others. Further inquiry should include a broader perspective of audience effects, advancing the theory to include the repercussions of the debatability of racism.
